cycles.name: Foundation Forge
focuses: Build foundational strength across lower body, upper body, and core with corrective priorities.
Establish movement patterns that counteract tennis-specific postural imbalances.
Develop scapular stability and posterior chain strength as injury-prevention anchors.
Introduce reactive plyometric work to build tendon elasticity and court-transfer qualities.

paragraphs: Mehraneh, this first cycle is not about pushing limits — it's about building the platform everything else stands on. Your body has been absorbing the demands of professional tennis for years, and right now the goal is to correct the imbalances that sport has created: rounded shoulders, compressed upper back, underloaded posterior chain. Every session in this block is designed to pull your posture back into alignment and build the muscular armor that keeps you on the court.

Mentally, stay patient with the process. The loads are intentionally moderate — not because you can't handle more, but because quality of movement is the actual training stimulus right now. Every rep done with control is a deposit into your long-term athletic account.

By the end of these five weeks, expect your upper back tension to reduce noticeably, your single-leg stability to feel significantly more solid, and your baseline strength across push, pull, and hinge patterns to be measurably higher than day one.
outcomes: Noticeable reduction in upper back tension and between-scapula discomfort
Measurably improved single-leg stability in split squat and step-down
Stronger and more controlled pulling patterns — rows, lat pull-down, band work
Improved postural awareness and scapular control during pressing movements
Consistent plyometric output with better ground contact quality
Established gym routine that fits training alongside tennis schedule

cycles.name: Strength Engine
focuses: Progressive overload across all major patterns — increase load and training density.
Deepen scapular stability and posterior chain strength as injury-prevention anchors.

teaser.subtitle: You've built the foundation. Now we load it.
paragraphs: Cycle 2 is where we start to load it with intent. The corrective work you've done becomes the base for something heavier, denser, and more demanding. The patterns are the same — the stimulus is not. Your body will be asked to produce more, recover faster, and adapt under real training pressure.
Earn it in these five weeks.

cycles.name: Power Transfer
focuses: Convert built strength into court-relevant power — rotational, reactive, and unilateral.
Reinforce shoulder girdle resilience under higher velocity demands.

cycles.name: Performance Peak
focuses: Sharpen athletic output — speed, reactive strength, and sport-specific conditioning.
Sustain structural integrity under competition load; refine movement economy.

cycles.name: Armor & Maintain
focuses: Maintain peak strength while reducing injury risk heading into high-competition periods.
Optimize recovery capacity and tissue resilience for the long season.

days.completionMessage: Strong session — recover well and hydrate.

good: Keep both sit bones heavy on the floor
bad: Don't let the pelvis rock side to side

good: Sit into the descent like you're lowering onto a chair
bad: Don't let the heels rise off the floor

good: Drive the knee forward over the pinky toe
bad: Don't let the heel lift during the drive

good: Think of the ankles as springs — fast and stiff off the floor
bad: Don't bend the knees deeply; keep the contact time short

good: Stay light — imagine the floor is hot
bad: Don't let the landing knee collapse inward

good: Push the floor apart under your feet as you drive up
bad: Don't let the chest collapse forward in the bottom position

good: Keep the torso upright — like a flagpole through your spine
bad: Don't let the front knee drift past the toes aggressively

good: Reach the free foot toward the floor with control — own the descent
bad: Don't let the standing knee cave inward as you lower

good: Rise through the big toe side — full range at the top
bad: Don't rush through the bottom — own the full stretch

good: Imagine someone is trying to rotate your hips — resist them completely
bad: Don't hold your breath; breathe steadily through the hold

days.completionMessage: Your shoulders thank you — great pulling work today.

exercises.name: Arm Bike / Ski Erg / Cross Trainer

good: Pull the band to your chest with your rear delts — not your arms
bad: Don't shrug the shoulders up as you pull

good: Think of spreading your shoulder blades as wide as possible at the top
bad: Don't let the elbows bend — this is pure scapular movement

good: Keep the elbow pinned to the side like it's glued there
bad: Don't let the shoulder blade wing out as you rotate

good: Press the floor away with the down knee to brace the whole body
bad: Don't arch the lower back to get the weight overhead

good: Drive the elbow back toward your hip pocket
bad: Don't rotate the torso to help — keep the hips square

good: Drive your upper back into the bench as you press
bad: Don't let the elbows flare out past 45° from the torso

good: Pull the bar toward your upper chest — think elbows to hips
bad: Don't lean back excessively to initiate the pull

good: Slow and deliberate — this is rehab work, not a strength exercise
bad: Don't use momentum; if you can't control it, the weight is too heavy

good: Squeeze the lower traps — imagine pinching a pen between your shoulder blades at the top
bad: Don't shrug the neck; keep ears away from shoulders throughout

good: Walk tall — imagine a string pulling the crown of your head to the ceiling
bad: Don't let the loaded shoulder hike up toward your ear

days.completionMessage: Full body session complete — excellent work.

good: Step long and drive through the front heel to stand
bad: Don't let the torso lean forward over the front knee

good: Keep the hips level — imagine a cup of water on your lower back
bad: Don't let the free hip drop below the standing hip

good: Press your lower back firmly into the floor throughout
bad: Don't let the lower back arch as the limbs extend

good: Land softly, then immediately explode back up — minimize ground contact time
bad: Don't pause on the box; the rebound is the stimulus

good: Think of the ankle as a piston — stiff and fast off the ground
bad: Don't let the knee buckle inward on any contact

good: Use the momentum from the squat to initiate the press — fluid transition
bad: Don't hyperextend the lower back at the top of the press

good: Hinge like a door — the hips are the hinge, the spine stays neutral
bad: Don't let the pelvis rotate open toward the free leg side

good: Drive from the hips and core — arms are just the link to the cable
bad: Don't let the movement come from the lower back rotating

good: Keep the hips perfectly level as each leg lifts — zero sway
bad: Don't let the lower back sag as fatigue builds

days.completionMessage: Zone 2 complete — this is where your engine is built.

good: Open the chest toward the ceiling on the rotation — maximize thoracic range
bad: Don't rush the movement; own each position for a full breath

good: Keep both sit bones grounded as you rotate
bad: Don't let the pelvis tilt to compensate for limited hip range

good: Move segment by segment — think of a wave traveling up the spine
bad: Don't collapse through one area; distribute the movement evenly

good: Follow your elbow with your eyes — maximize the rotation
bad: Don't let the hips rotate along with the upper back

good: Rotate the torso fully over the front leg on each step
bad: Don't let the knee collapse inward as you lunge

good: Drive the knee forward without lifting the heel
bad: Don't let the arch of the foot collapse inward

good: Exhale fully as you extend — use the breath to brace
bad: Don't let the lower back lift off the floor at any point

good: Build into the effort in the first 5 seconds — don't spike immediately
bad: Don't go all-out; you have 14 more rounds to survive

good: Use this minute to fully bring your breathing back under control
bad: Don't stop completely — keep moving to clear lactate faster

cards.title: You Need More Fuel Than You Think
cards.body: You are running a professional athlete's training load on insufficient calories. This is the single biggest threat to the results this program is designed to deliver. Undereating at this stress level suppresses muscle protein synthesis, elevates cortisol, and actively works against the injury prevention goal you're training for. You don't need to overhaul your diet overnight — but prioritize protein at every meal (aim for at least 1.6g per kg of bodyweight daily) and treat food as part of your training, not separate from it.

cards.title: High Stress Changes How You Should Train
cards.body: With a stress level of 8, your nervous system is already carrying a significant load before you walk into the gym. This means your perceived exertion will run higher than your actual output on tough days — trust the RPE targets and don't chase numbers when your body is signaling fatigue. On high-stress days, the conditioning sessions are your best friend: 30 minutes of Zone 2 cardio is one of the most effective stress-regulation tools available to you.

cards.title: Your Upper Back Is the Priority
cards.body: The pain between your shoulder blades and your rounded posture are directly connected to years of tennis — dominant internal rotation, heavy overhead load, and anterior chain tightness. Every pulling movement in this program (rows, lat pull-downs, band pull-aparts) is selected specifically to reverse this pattern. The cable external rotation and Y-raise work in Day 2 are not accessories — they are the most important exercises in this cycle for your long-term shoulder health.

cards.title: Single-Leg Work Is Your Injury Insurance
cards.body: Tennis is almost entirely a single-leg sport — every change of direction, every split step, every lateral push-off loads one leg at a time. The split squats, step-downs, single-leg RDLs, and single-leg pogos in this program are building exactly the hip stability and knee control that prevent the ankle, knee, and hip injuries most common in professional players. Do not rush the reps or compensate with the trunk — the difficulty you feel here is the adaptation you need.

cards.title: Plyometrics First — Always
cards.body: The pogo jumps, lateral hops, and box rebounds are placed early in sessions deliberately. Plyometric quality degrades rapidly with fatigue, and the neural adaptations they produce — reactive stiffness, tendon elasticity, ground contact efficiency — are only accessible when the nervous system is fresh. Do not skip or swap the order. Even when you feel like going straight to the weights, the jump work is building the reactive foundation that transfers directly to your first step on court.

cards.title: Sleep Is the Adaptation
cards.body: A sleep score of 7 is workable — but at stress level 8 and low caloric intake, you have very little recovery buffer. The muscle you're trying to build is not built in the gym — it's built in the hours after. Protect your sleep window aggressively: consistent bed and wake times, no screens 30 minutes before sleep, and treat the conditioning days as active recovery tools that will improve sleep quality over time.
